name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱصْبِرُوا۟ وَصَابِرُوا۟ وَرَابِطُوا۟ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٢٠٠)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be patient, stand firm, and close [your] ranks, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous. (200)
```

## (Commentary)

The verse serves as a summary (الفذلكة) of the detailed explanation provided in the Surah, concluding it by extracting the result and presenting it.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اصْبِرُوا وَصابِرُوا** — "O you who have faith! Be patient, and stand firm" (3:200) etc. The commands are absolute; thus, patience (الصبر) refers to patience in the face of hardships, patience in the obedience of Allah, and patience in refraining from His disobedience. In any case, it refers to the patience of the individual, as evidenced by the term contrasted with it.

**Standing firm (المصابرة)** — *the act of collective endurance and bearing harm as a group* — is based on the patience of some relying on the patience of others. Through this, the state is strengthened, the quality intensified, and its impact multiplied. This is a perceptible matter regarding the influence of the individual when his personality is considered in isolation versus when considered in a state of social gathering and cooperation, where forces are joined together. We shall discuss this extensively in its proper place, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَرابِطُوا** — "and be ever ready" (3:200) — is broader in meaning than standing firm (المصابرة). It refers to the formation of a community (الجماعة) and the interconnection (الارتباط) between their powers and actions in all affairs of their religious life, whether in times of hardship or ease. Since the objective of this is to attain the reality of the intended happiness (السعادة) for this world and the Hereafter — otherwise, only a partial worldly happiness would be achieved, which is not true happiness — He followed these commands with His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** — "and be God-conscious, so that you may be felicitous" (3:200), meaning complete and true felicity (الفلاح).

## (A Discourse on Social Bond in Islamic Society)

**1 — Man and Society:** The fact that the human species is a social species (نوع اجتماعي) does not require much research to prove. Every individual of this species is created with an innate disposition (مفطور) toward this. Man has always lived in a state of social gathering, as narrated by history and the observable remains that tell of the most ancient eras in which this species lived and ruled upon this earth.

The Quran has informed us of this in the best possible manner in many verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا** — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another" (49:13). And He, the Exalted, said: **نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَرَفَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا** — "It is We who dispense their livelihood between them in the present life, and We elevate some of them above others in rank, so that some of them may employ others in service" (43:32). And He, the Exalted, said: **بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ** — "You are all [members] of one another" (3:195). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَصِهْراً** — "It is He who created the human being from water, then He established for him lineage and kinship" (25:54), among other verses. (1)

**2 — Man and His Growth within Society:** Human society, like all other human spiritual properties (الخواص الروحية) and what pertains to them, did not come into existence in a complete and perfect state that accepts no growth or increase. Rather, like all other human spiritual and cognitive matters, it has continued to perfect itself alongside the perfection of man in his material and spiritual attainments. In truth, it was not to be expected that this property would be an exception among all human properties, appearing at its first emergence as complete and perfect as it could possibly be. Instead, like other human properties linked to the faculties of knowledge (العلم) and will (الإرادة), it attains perfection gradually in man. What appears from reflecting upon the state of this species is that the first form of social gathering to appear was domestic society (الاجتماع المنزلي) through marriage. This is because its natural driver — the reproductive system — is the strongest factor of social gathering, as it cannot be realized by a single individual at all, unlike nutrition and other needs.

Subsequently, there emerged from it the property we named in previous discussions of this book as "utilization" (الاستخدام) — *the act of man employing others as intermediaries to fulfill his needs by extending his authority and imposing his will upon them.* This then manifested in the form of leadership (الرئاسة), such as the head of the household, the head of the clan, the head of the tribe, and the head of the nation. Naturally, the designated leader among a group was initially the strongest and bravest, then the bravest and most abundant in wealth and children, and so on, until it culminated in the one most knowledgeable in the arts of governance and politics. This was the primary cause for the emergence of idolatry (الوثنية) and its persistence to this day. We shall provide a full discussion of this in what is to come, Allah the Mighty willing.

Although the property of social gathering in all its forms (domestic and otherwise) never left humanity during these stages for even a moment, it was not consciously recognized by man in detail. Rather, it lived and grew as a byproduct of other properties that man was concerned with, such as utilization (الاستخدام) and defense (الدفاع) and the like.

The Noble Quran informs us that the first to alert man to social gathering in detail and to care for its preservation independently was Prophethood (النبوة). He, the Exalted, said: **وَما كانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا** — "Mankind were but a single community; then they differed" (10:19). And He said: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** — "Mankind were but one community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed" (2:213). This informs us that man in his most ancient eras was a single, simple community (أمة واحدة) with no differences among them, until differences emerged and disputes became apparent. Then Allah sent the prophets and sent down the Book with them to remove the difference and return them to a social unity preserved by legislated laws.

He, the Exalted, said: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus: 'Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it'" (42:13). Thus, He informed us that the removal of differences among people and the creation of unity in their word was only in the form of a call to establish the religion and not to be divided in it. Therefore, religion (الدين) was the guarantor of their righteous social gathering.

The verse — as you see — attributes this call (the call to social gathering and unity) to Noah (peace be upon him), who is the most ancient of the prophets possessing a law (شريعة) and a Book, then to Abraham, then to Moses, then to Jesus (peace be upon them).

The laws of Noah and Abraham contained a small number of rulings. The most extensive of these four was the law of Moses, followed by the law of Jesus, as reported by the Quran and as is evident in the Gospels. It is said that the law of Moses contains only about six hundred rulings.

Thus, the call to social gathering did not begin as an independent and explicit call except from the direction of Prophethood (النبوة) in the mold of religion, as the Quran explicitly states and as history confirms, as will be discussed.

**3 — Islam and Its Concern for Social Gathering:** There is no doubt that Islam is the only religion that explicitly founded its structure upon social gathering (الاجتماع). It did not neglect the matter of social gathering in any of its affairs. If you wish for further insight into this, look at the vastness of human actions that the mind is unable to count, and their branching into genera, species, and categories. Then look at how this divine law (الشريعة) has enumerated them, encompassed them, and extended its rulings over them; you will see something wondrous. Then look at how it cast all of that into the mold of social gathering; you will see that it infused the spirit of social gathering (روح الاجتماع) into them to the greatest possible extent.

Then proceed to compare what you have found with other true laws that the Quran cares for — namely the laws of Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus — so that you may witness the proportion and know the status.

As for the laws that the Noble Quran does not care for, such as the religions of idolatry (الوثنية), the Sabians (الصابئة), Manichaeism (المانوية), Dualism (الثنوية), and others, the matter is even more evident and clear.

As for the civilized nations and others, history mentions nothing of their affairs except that they followed what they inherited from the most ancient eras of humanity: the subordination of social gathering to utilization (الاستخدام), and the gathering of individuals under the umbrella of autocratic government and monarchical power. Thus, ethnic, national, and regional social gathering lived under the banner of the king and leadership, guided by the factors of heredity, geography, and others, without any of these nations giving independent concern to its affair or making it a subject of research and action. Even the great nations that held world sovereignty when the sun of religion rose and began its illumination — namely the Roman and Persian Empires — were nothing but Caesarism and Khosrowism. Their nations gathered under the banner of the king and sultanate, and social gathering followed in its growth and development, remaining as long as they remained.

Yes, there exist in what they inherited social discussions in the writings of their philosophers, such as Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, and others. However, these were merely papers and scrolls that did not enter the realm of action; they were mental ideals (مثلا ذهنية) that did not descend to the stage of external reality (العين والخارج). Inherited history is the fairest witness to the truth of what we have mentioned.

The first call to strike the ears of the human species, inviting this species to care for the matter of social gathering by making it an independent subject removed from the corner of neglect and the rule of subordination, was that proclaimed by the Proclaimer of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He called the people, through the verses of his Lord revealed to him, to the happiness of life and a goodly existence in a state of social gathering. He, the Exalted, said: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ** — "This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow other ways, for they will separate you from His way" (6:153). And He said: **وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً وَلا تَفَرَّقُوا** — "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided" (3:103), until He said: **وَلْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** — "There has to be a nation among you summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong" (3:104) — referring to preserving society from division and branching — **وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ تَفَرَّقُوا وَاخْتَلَفُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ** — "it is they who are the felicitous. Do not be like those who became divided [into sects] and differed after manifest signs had come to them" (3:104-105). And He said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ** — "Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you will not have anything to do with them" (6:159), among other absolute verses calling to the principle of social gathering and unity.

He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ** — "The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers" (49:10). And He said: **وَلا تَنازَعُوا فَتَفْشَلُوا وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ** — "and do not dispute, or you will lose heart and your power will be gone" (8:46). And He said: **وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبرِّ وَالتَّقْوى** — "and help one another in piety and God-consciousness" (5:2). And He said: **وَلْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** — "There has to be a nation among you summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong" (3:104), among other verses commanding the building of Islamic society upon agreement and unity in acquiring its spiritual and material benefits and advantages, and defending it, as we shall clarify to some extent.

**4 — Islam's Consideration of the Bond between the Individual and Society:** Creation and origination (الصنع والإيجاد) first produce primary parts with specific effects and properties, then combine and harmonize them despite their points of distinction. From this, new benefits are derived in addition to the observable benefits of the parts. For example, man has parts, portions, limbs, and faculties that have diverse material and spiritual benefits. Sometimes they harmonize, becoming strong and great, such as the weight of each part and the weight of the whole, or the ability to move from one direction to another. Sometimes they do not harmonize and remain in a state of disparity and separation, such as hearing, sight, taste, will, and movement. However, they are all, from the perspective of unity in composition, under the control of the single emergent entity — which is the human being. At that point, benefits arise that do not exist in any of his individual parts; these are numerous benefits of the nature of action, passion (الفعل والانفعال), and spiritual and material advantages. Among his benefits is the occurrence of a wondrous multiplicity within those benefits while maintaining unity. For instance, human matter, such as the sperm (النطفة), when its development is complete, is capable of secreting a portion of matter from itself and nurturing it into another complete human being who performs material and spiritual acts similar to those of his origin and source. Thus, human individuals, despite their multiplicity, are "man," which is one; and their actions are many in number but one in species (نوع). They gather and harmonize like water divided into vessels; they are many waters of one species, possessing many properties of one species. Whenever the waters are gathered in one place, the property is strengthened and the effect becomes great.

In the education of the individuals of this species and their guidance toward their true happiness, Islam has considered this real meaning within them, and there is no escape from considering it. He, the Exalted, said: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَصِهْراً** — "It is He who created the human being from water, then He established for him lineage and kinship" (25:54). And He said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى** — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female" (49:13). And He said: **بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ** — "You are all [members] of one another" (3:195).

This real bond (الرابطة الحقيقية) between the person and society inevitably leads to another mode of being (كينونة) in society, according to what individuals provide of their existence, powers, properties, and effects. Thus, there is formed in society a nature similar to that of the individual's existence and the properties of existence, which is clearly observable. Therefore, the Quran has attributed to the nation (الأمة) an existence, a term (أجل), a book, consciousness (شعور), understanding, action, obedience, and disobedience. He said: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ** — "There is a time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it" (7:34). And He said: **كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا** — "Every nation will be summoned to its book" (45:28). And He said: **زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ** — "We have made every nation’s deeds seem goodly to them" (6:108). And He said: **مِنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُقْتَصِدَةٌ** — "Among them are a moderate community" (5:66). And He said: **أُمَّةٌ قائِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ آياتِ اللهِ** — "an upright community, who recite Allah’s signs" (3:113). And He said: **وَهَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ وَجادَلُوا بِالْباطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا بِهِ الْحَقَّ فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ عِقابِ** — "Every nation conspired against its messenger to seize him, and they disputed by means of falsehood to refute the truth therewith; so I seized them, and how was My retribution!" (40:5). And He said: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ** — "There is a messenger for every nation: when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice" (10:47).

From here, we see that the Quran cares for the histories of nations just as it cares for the stories of individuals, or even more so, at a time when histories only recorded the conditions of famous kings and great men. Historians did not engage with the histories of nations and societies until after the revelation of the Quran. Some individuals, such as al-Mas'udi and Ibn Khaldun, engaged with it to some extent, until the final transformation occurred in transmitted history by replacing individuals with nations. The first to establish this, as is said, was the Frenchman Auguste Comte, who died in 1857 AD.

In short, the necessary consequence of this, as previously indicated, is the formation of powerful social forces and properties that overwhelm individual forces and properties when they conflict or contradict. Moreover, sense perception and experience testify to this in both active and passive forces and properties. The resolve and will of a group in a matter — as in cases of mobs and social surges — cannot be stood against by any opposing or contradicting will from any of its individuals or parts. The part has no choice but to follow its whole and proceed as it proceeds, to the point that consciousness and thought are stripped from its individuals and parts. Similarly, general fear and general panic — as in cases of defeat, loss of security, earthquakes, famine, and plague, or lesser things like customary rituals and national fashions — compel the individual to follow and strip him of the power of perception and thought.

This is the criterion for Islam's intense concern for the matter of social gathering — a concern the likes of which we do not find, and shall never find, in any other religion or in the traditions of civilized nations (and perhaps you can hardly believe that). For the cultivation of ethics and instincts in the individual — which is the basis for the existence of society — can hardly succeed when opposing and contradicting ethics and instincts are powerful and dominant in society, except to a negligible degree when measured and estimated.

Thus, it placed its most important rulings and laws, such as the Pilgrimage (الحج), Prayer (الصلاة), Struggle (الجهاد), and Spending (الإنفاق) — in short, religious God-consciousness (التقوي الديني) — upon the foundation of social gathering. It maintained this, in addition to the powers of the Islamic government that preserve the public religious rites and their boundaries, and in addition to the obligation of summoning to good and bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong, which is general to the entire nation. It made the goal of Islamic society — and no society can do without a common goal — true happiness and proximity and status with Allah. This is an internal sentinel (رقيب باطني) from whom nothing in the human conscience or secret is hidden — let alone what is manifest — even if it is hidden from the group of callers and those who bid what is right and forbid what is wrong. This is what we meant when we said that Islam's concern for social gathering surpasses all other traditions and ways.

**5 — Is the Islamic Social Tradition Capable of Implementation and Survival?** You might say: If what has been mentioned is true — that Islam's vision in forming a righteous society is of a higher structure and more solid foundation than even the societies formed by truly advanced civilized nations — then why was it only implemented for a short period, and then could not prevent itself from being transformed into Caesarism and Khosrowism, turning into an empire whose actions were more tragic and hideous than what preceded it, unlike Western civilization which sustains its survival?

This is the evidence that their civilization is more advanced and their social tradition more solid and robust. They have placed their social tradition and current laws upon the foundation of the nation's will and the suggestion of natural dispositions and inclinations. Then they considered the will of the majority and their suggestion, due to the habitual impossibility of the entire group agreeing on a single will. The dominance of the majority is a current tradition in nature that is observable; for we find that each of the material causes and natural reasons is effective in the majority of cases, not always. Similarly, among various conflicting factors, only the majority are effective, not all and not the minority. Thus, it is appropriate that the structure of society be built, according to its purpose and the traditions and laws current within it, upon the will of the majority. As for the religious hypothesis, in the present world, it is nothing but a wish that does not exceed the stage of hypothesis and a mental ideal that is not permissible to attain.

Modern civilization has guaranteed, in the countries where it has appeared, the strength of society and its happiness, and the refinement of individuals and their purification from vices — those matters that society does not approve of, such as lying, betrayal, injustice, harshness, and the like.

This which we have presented is the summary of what occurs in the minds of many of our Eastern researchers, especially the educated among our thinkers who reflect on social and psychological matters. However, they approached this research from the wrong direction, and thus the truth of the matter became confused for them. To clarify this, we say:

As for their saying that the Islamic social tradition is not capable of being practiced in the world, unlike the traditions of modern civilization in the context of existing conditions — meaning that present world conditions do not harmonize with the laws legislated in Islam — this is granted, but it yields nothing. For all the traditions current in the human community only came into being after they were not, and appeared at a time when the general existing conditions and circumstances were nothing but contradictory to them and repelling them. They then rose up and contended with the preceding, long-standing, and deep-rooted traditions. Sometimes they were oppressed and defeated in their first uprising, then returned a second and third time until they triumphed, became established, and held sway. Sometimes they perished and became extinct if factors and conditions did not support them afterward. History testifies (1) to this in all traditions, religious and worldly, even in things like democracy and socialism. To the likes of this, His saying, the Exalted, refers: **قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُروا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ** — "Certain customs have passed before you; so travel through the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers" (3:137), indicating that the tradition accompanied by the denial of Allah's signs does not end in a good or praiseworthy outcome.

Thus, the mere lack of correspondence of a tradition with the present human condition does not reveal its falsehood or corruption. Rather, it is among the natural traditions current in the world for completing the formation of new events following action, passion, and the conflict of various factors.

Islam, like other traditions from the perspective of natural and social observation, is not an exception to this universal rule. Its state in terms of advancement, delay, and seeking support from factors and conditions is the state of all other traditions. The state of Islam today — having become established in the souls of more than four hundred million human individuals and taken root in their hearts — is not weaker than its state in the world during the time of the calls of Noah, Abraham, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), when the call of each of them stood by a single person and the world at that time knew nothing but corruption. Then it expanded, took root, lived, and joined one with another, and has not been severed until today.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood with the call when there was no one to support him that day except a man and a woman. Then they continued to be joined by one after another, and today is a day of great hardship, until the help of Allah came to them. They formed a righteous society whose individuals were dominated by righteousness and God-consciousness. They remained in social righteousness for a period until the trials occurred after the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

This small model, despite its short life and narrow scope, did not delay in expanding in less than half a century over the east and west of the earth. It transformed history in a fundamental way whose significant effects are witnessed to our day and shall continue.

Social and psychological research in theoretical history cannot refrain from admitting that the proximate origin and complete factor for the contemporary transformation witnessed in the world is the emergence and rising of the Islamic tradition. Most researchers from Europe have only neglected to fully investigate its impact on the human community due to religious prejudice or political reasons. How can an expert researcher — if he were to look fairly — call the modern civilized movement a "Christian movement" and consider Jesus (peace be upon him) its leader and standard-bearer, when Jesus explicitly states (1) that he only cares for the matter of the spirit and does not engage in the matter of the body, nor does he interfere in the affairs of the state and politics? Yet here is Islam, calling to social gathering and harmony, and intervening in all affairs of human society and its individuals without exception. Is this overlooking and ignoring on their part anything other than an attempt to extinguish the light of Allah — **وَيأْبَى اللهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يُتِمَّ نُورَهُ** — "but Allah refuses but to perfect His light" (9:32) — and to dampen its fire in the hearts out of envy and enmity, until it becomes a mere ethnicity with no effect except that of branched lineages?

In short, Islam has proven its suitability for guiding people to their happiness and a goodly life. That which possesses this quality is not called a hypothesis incapable of being applied to human life, nor is it to be despaired of its governance over the affairs of the world one day (given that its objective is the true happiness of man). It has already been mentioned in the commentary on the verse: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً** — "Mankind were but one community" (2:213) — that deep research into the conditions of cosmic existents leads to the conclusion that the human species will reach its goal and attain its desire, which is the perfection of the manifestation of Islam in its reality in the world and its complete governance over the affairs of human society. Allah the Exalted has promised this according to this theory in His Noble Book, saying: **فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ** — "Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, dignified before the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer" (5:54). And He said: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْناً يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً** — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely replace their fear with security: 'They shall worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me'" (24:55). And He said: **أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ** — "that My righteous servants shall inherit the earth" (21:105), among other verses.

There is another aspect that these people have overlooked in their research, which is that the sole slogan of Islamic society is the following of truth (الحق) in thought and action, while the slogan of modern civilized society is the following of what the majority sees and desires. These two slogans necessitate a difference in the goal of the formed society. The goal of Islamic society is true intellectual happiness (السعادة الحقيقية العقلية), in the sense that man adopts moderation in the requirements of his faculties, giving the body its desires to the extent that it does not hinder him from knowing Allah through the path of servitude (العبودية); rather, it becomes a preliminary that leads to it. In this lies the happiness of man through the happiness of all his faculties, and it is the great rest (though we do not truly perceive it today due to the disruption of Islamic education among us). Therefore, Islam placed its laws upon the foundation of observing the side of the intellect (العقل), which is naturally disposed to follow the truth. It strictly prohibited what corrupts the sound intellect and placed the guarantee for the implementation of all actions, ethics, and primary knowledge upon the responsibility of society, in addition to what the Islamic government and guardianship (الولاية) maintain in terms of implementing policies, legal punishments (الحدود), and others. This, in any case, does not suit the natural dispositions of the common people and is repelled by this wondrous immersion in whims and desires that we witness from all the affluent and the destitute alike. It strips them of their freedom in pleasure-seeking, amusement, predatory behavior, and aggression, except after intense struggle in spreading the call and extending education, just like all other high matters that man needs a decisive resolve, sufficient training, and constant preservation to adopt.

As for the goal of modern civilized society, it is the enjoyment of matter (المادة). It is clear that this entails a sensory life (حياة إحساسية) that follows what the natural disposition inclines toward, whether it agrees with what is true according to the intellect or not. Rather, the intellect is only followed in what does not contradict its goal and purpose.

Therefore, the laws in their formulation and implementation follow what the whims of the majority of society and their natural inclinations demand. The guarantee of implementation is confined to the articles of law pertaining to actions. As for ethics and primary knowledge, there is no guarantor for their implementation; rather, people are free to adopt them or not, unless they interfere with the law in its course, in which case they are prevented.

The necessary consequence of this is that the society whose state is such becomes accustomed to what agrees with its whims of the vices of lust and anger. Thus, it deems good much of what religion used to deem ugly. It indulges in playing with virtuous ethics and high knowledge, supported by legal freedom.

The consequence of this consequence is that the mode of thought transforms from the intellectual course to the sensory and emotional course. Thus, what might be debauchery and profligacy in the course of the intellect becomes piety in the course of inclinations and sensations, and is called gallantry, cheerfulness, and good character — like most of what occurs in Europe among the youth, between men and chaste women or virgins, between women and dogs, between men and their children and close relatives, and what occurs in celebrations, dance halls, and other things that the tongue of one disciplined by the etiquette of religion shrinks from mentioning.

Sometimes the common practices of the religious path become strange and laughably wondrous to them, and vice versa. All of this is due to the difference in the mode of thought and perception resulting from the difference in the path. In these sensory traditions, nothing is utilized from intellection (التعقل) — as you have known — except the amount that levels the path to enjoyment and pleasure. For that is the sole goal that nothing opposes and nothing prevents, except in the case of conflict with its like, to the point that you find among the current draft laws things like "suicide" and "dueling" and others. For the soul is what it wants and desires, unless it interferes with what the society wants and desires!

If you reflect on this difference, the reason for the greater suitability of the Western social tradition to the taste of the human community over the religious social tradition will become clear to you. However, it must be remembered that the Western civilized tradition alone is not what agrees with the natural dispositions of people such that it should be preferred by that alone. Rather, all the practiced traditions current in the world among its people, from the most ancient eras of humanity to our era — from the traditions of nomadism and civilization — share the fact that people prefer them over the religion that calls to the truth when they are first presented with them, due to their submission to material idolatry.

If you were to reflect truly, you would find that this present civilization is nothing but composed of the traditions of the first idolatry, except that it has transformed from a state of individualism to a state of social gathering, and from a stage of simplicity to a stage of technical precision.

What we have mentioned regarding the building of the Islamic tradition upon the following of truth (الحق) rather than the agreement with natural disposition is among the clearest of matters in the Quranic statements. He, the Exalted, said: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِ** — "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the religion of truth" (9:33). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَاللهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِ** — "and Allah judges with the truth" (40:20). And He said in describing the believers: **وَتَواصَوْا بِالْحَقِ** — "and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth" (103:3). And He said: **لَقَدْ جِئْناكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ** — "We have certainly brought you the truth, but most of you were averse to the truth" (43:78). Thus, He admitted that the truth does not agree with the natural dispositions and whims of the majority. Then He rejected the necessity of agreeing with the whims of the majority by stating that it leads to corruption, saying: **بَلْ جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ ، وَلَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْواءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ بَلْ أَتَيْناهُمْ بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ** — "Rather, he has brought them the truth, and most of them are averse to the truth. Had the truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have surely fallen apart [along] with whoever is in them. Rather, We have brought them their reminder, but they are disregardful of their reminder" (23:70-71). Indeed, the course of events and the accumulation of corruption day by day have confirmed what He, the Exalted, explained in this verse. And He, the Exalted, said: **فَما ذا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلالُ فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ** — "So what is there after the truth except error? Then where are you being turned away?" (10:32). The verses with this meaning and what is close to it are very many. If you wish for further insight into this, refer to Surah Yunus, for the mention of truth (الحق) is repeated in it more than twenty times.

As for their saying that following the majority is a current tradition in nature, there is no doubt that nature follows the majority in its effects. However, it is not such that it nullifies or contradicts the obligation of following the truth; for nature itself is one of the instances of truth. How then could it nullify itself? The clarification of this requires the explanation of several matters:

First: External matters, which are the foundations of man's scientific and practical beliefs, follow in their formation and types of transformation the system of causality (العلية والمعلولية). This is a permanent, fixed system that accepts no exception. The accomplished scholars of knowledge and observation have agreed upon this, and the Quran has testified to it as previously mentioned (1). Thus, the external course does not deviate from permanence and stability, to the point that events of majority occurrence (الحوادث الأكثرية), which are relative, are permanent and fixed in their being majority occurrences. For example, fire, which produces heat in most cases relative to all its instances — its "majority heating" is a permanent effect for it, and so on. This is the truth (الحق).

Second: Man, according to his primordial nature (الفطرة), follows what he finds to be an external, factual matter in some way. Thus, he follows the truth according to his primordial nature, to the point that even one who denies the existence of certain knowledge, if a statement is presented to him in which he finds no hesitation within himself, he submits to it with acceptance.

Third: Truth (الحق), as you have known, is the external matter to which man submits in his belief or follows in his action. As for man's view and perception, it is merely a means he uses to reach it, like a mirror in relation to the object seen.

When you know these matters, it becomes clear to you that "truthness" (الحقية) — which is the permanence of occurrence or the majority of occurrence in nature, which also returns to permanence and stability — is a quality of the external reality that occurs permanently or in the majority of cases, not a quality of knowledge and perception. In other words, it is a quality of the known matter (المعلوم), not a quality of the knowledge (العلم). Thus, permanent and majority occurrence is also, in a way, the truth. As for the opinions, views, and beliefs of the majority as opposed to the minority, they are not always the truth. Rather, they might be the truth if they correspond to reality, and they might not be if they do not correspond. In that case, it is not appropriate for man to submit to them, nor would he submit to them if he were alert to reality. For if you are certain of a matter and then all people oppose you in it, you do not naturally submit to their view, even if you follow them in it outwardly. You only follow them out of fear, shame, or another factor, not because it is a truth that must be followed in itself. Among the best explanations that the opinion and view of the majority need not be a truth that must be followed is His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ** — "Rather, he has brought them the truth, and most of them are averse to the truth" (23:70). If everything the majority sees were the truth, it would not be possible for them to hate the truth and oppose it.

By this explanation, the falsity of basing the following of the majority upon the tradition of nature becomes apparent. For this tradition is current in the external reality to which knowledge pertains, not in the knowledge and thought itself. What man follows of this tradition in his will and movements is only the majority of occurrence in the external reality, not what the majority believed. That is, he builds his acts and deeds upon the majority benefit (الصلاح الأكثري). Upon this, the Quran proceeded in the ruling of its legislations and their interests. He, the Exalted, said: **ما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ حَرَجٍ وَلكِنْ يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — "Allah does not desire to put you to hardship, but He desires to purify you, and to complete His blessing upon you so that you may give thanks" (5:6). And He, the Exalted, said: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيامُ كَما كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** — "fasting has been prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you, so that you may be God-conscious" (2:183), among other verses containing the criteria of majority occurrence for the legislated rulings.

As for their saying that modern civilization has granted advanced nations the happiness of society and refined individuals, purifying them from vices that society does not approve of, this is a statement not free from confusion and ambiguity. It seems their meaning of social happiness is the superiority of society in its numbers and strength, and its excellence in utilizing material resources. You have known repeatedly that Islam does not consider that happiness, and demonstrative research (البحث البرهاني) also supports this. Rather, human happiness is a matter composed of the happiness of the soul and the body. It is man's enjoyment of material blessings while being adorned with virtuous ethics and true divine knowledge. This is what guarantees his happiness in the life of this world and the life of the Hereafter. As for immersion in the pleasures of matter while neglecting the happiness of the spirit, it is nothing but misery (شقاء) in its view.

As for their wonder at what they see of honesty, clarity, trust, cheerfulness, and so on among the individuals of advanced nations, the reality of the matter has become confused for them. That is because most of the thinkers among our Eastern researchers are unable to think socially (التفكر الاجتماعي); they only think individually (تفكرا فرديا). What one of us sees before his eyes is that he is a human existent independent of all things, not linked to them by a bond that nullifies his existential independence (though the truth is otherwise). Then he does not think in his life except to bring benefits to himself and repel harms from himself. He only engages in his own affair, which is individual thinking. This entails that he measures others by himself, judging them as he judges in this manner of independence.

This judgment, if correct, is only correct for one who proceeds in his thinking along this course. As for one who thinks socially, he only sees before his eyes that he is an inseparable and non-independent part of society, and that his benefits are a part of the benefits of his society. He sees the good of society as his own good and its evil as his own evil, and every quality and state of it as a quality and state for himself. This person thinks in another way. He does not engage in relating to others except with those who are outside his society. As for his engagement with the parts of his society, he does not care for it nor does he value it as anything.

Clarify this with the example we provide: Man is a whole composed of many limbs and faculties. They all gather in a type of gathering that gives them a real unity we call "humanity." This necessitates the absorption of all of them, in essence and action, under his independence. Thus, the eye, the ear, the hand, and the foot see, hear, seize, and walk for the human being. Each only takes pleasure in its action within the pleasure of the human being in it. Each of these limbs and faculties is concerned with relating to the external world that the single human being wants to relate to, for good or evil. The eye, ear, hand, or foot only wants to do good or evil to the one the human being wants to do good or evil to among people, for example. As for the treatment of some of them with others while all are under the banner of the single humanity, it rarely happens that some of them do evil to others or some are harmed by others.

This is the state of the parts of man while they proceed in a single social course. In its ruling is the state of the individuals of a human society if they think socially. Their righteousness and God-consciousness, or their corruption and criminality, and their doing good and doing evil, are merely what their society possesses of these qualities when taken as a single personality. This is how the Quran acted in its judgment upon nations and peoples whom religious or ethnic prejudices compelled to think socially, such as the Jews, the Bedouins, and several past nations. You see it holding the later ones accountable for the sins of the earlier ones, and censuring the present ones and rebuking them for the actions of the absent and the past. All of that is because it is the true judgment for one who thinks socially. In the Noble Quran, there are many verses of this category; there is no need to quote them.

Yes, the requirement of fairness is not to oppress the right of the righteous among individuals by that, if they are found in a single society. For although they lived among them and mixed with them, their hearts were not defiled by the corrupt thought and the latent disease prevalent in such a society. Their persons are like extra parts in its structure and build. Thus, the Quran acted in the verses of general censure, excepting the righteous and the virtuous.

It becomes clear from what we have mentioned that the judgment of righteousness or wickedness upon the individuals of advanced civilized societies, unlike the individuals of other nations, should not be based on what appears from their social interaction and mixing among themselves and their internal life. Rather, it should be based on their prominent social personality in its contact and friction with other weak nations and its vital mixing with other social personalities in the world.

This is what must be observed and considered in judging the righteousness or wickedness of a society and its happiness or misery. Along this course our researchers must proceed; then, if they wish, let them be amazed, and if they wish, let them wonder.

By my life, if the reflecting observer were to study the history of their social life since the modern European renaissance and delve into how they treated other poor, weak nations and generations, he would not delay in seeing that these societies — which appear to be filled with compassion and sincerity for humanity, sacrificing blood and wealth for the sake of service to this species and giving freedom and helping the truly oppressed, and abolishing the tradition of slavery and captivity — he would see that they have no concern except to enslave the weak nations, the poor of the earth, whenever they find a way to do so, by whatever means they find. One day by force, one day by colonialism, one day by possession, one day by guardianship, one day in the name of preserving common interests, one day in the name of helping to preserve independence, one day in the name of preserving peace and repelling what threatens it, one day in the name of defending the rights of the eradicated and deprived classes, and one day... and one day....

Societies whose state is such — the sound human primordial nature (الفطرة) does not approve of describing them as righteous or conceding happiness to them, even if one were to overlook what the judgment of religion and the ruling of revelation and Prophethood identifies as the meaning of happiness.

How can the human nature agree to equip its individuals with what it equips them equally, then contradict itself by giving some of them a covenant to possess others — a possession that permits for them their blood, honor, and wealth, and levels the path for them to play with the core of their lives and existence and to dispose of their perception and will in a way that the human of the first centuries never encountered nor suffered? The reliance in all that we mention is the histories of the lives of these nations and what the present generation suffers at their hands. If what they have is called happiness and righteousness, then let it be in the sense of arbitrary control and the absolute exercise of whim.

**6 — By What is Islamic Society Formed and Sustained?** There is no doubt that society — any society — is only realized and obtained by the existence of a single common goal among its scattered individuals. This is the single spirit (الروح الواحدة) pervading all its parts by which they unite in a type of unity. This goal and purpose in the types of formed non-religious societies is merely the goal of the worldly life for man, but in the manner of partnership among individuals, not in the manner of isolation. It is the enjoyment of the advantages of material life in a social manner.

The difference between social and individual enjoyment in terms of property is that if man were able to live alone, he would be free-reined in each of his enjoyments, as he would have no opponent or sentinel except what some of his systems restrict others. For he cannot inhale all the air, as the lungs cannot contain it even if he desired it; nor can he eat of food materials without limit, as the digestive system cannot bear it. This is his state in measuring some of his powers and limbs against others. As for in relation to another human being like him, since there is no partner for him in what he benefits from matter by hypothesis, there is no cause there necessitating the narrowing of his field of action, nor the limitation of any of his acts and deeds.

This is unlike the human being situated in the context and arena of society. For if he were free-reined in his will and actions, it would lead to mutual prevention and conflict in which there is the corruption of life and the destruction of the species. We have explained this in the previous discussions on Prophethood in the most complete manner.

This is the sole reason that necessitates the governance of law within society. However, primitive societies (المجتمعات الهمجية) do not become aware of their condition through deliberate thought and reflection; rather, their customs and norms are formed by the frequent quarrels and disputes among their members, which eventually compel everyone to observe certain matters that preserve their society to some extent. Since these norms are not built upon a firm foundation, they remain subject to violation and annulment, changing rapidly and eventually perishing. In contrast, civilized societies (المجتمعات المتمدنة) build their structure upon a sound foundation according to their degree of civility and culture. Through this, they eliminate the contradiction and mutual interference occurring between the individual wills and the collective actions of society by moderating them—placing limits and constraints upon them—and then concentrating power and authority in a center that guarantees the implementation of what the law dictates.

From this, several points become clear:

First: Law, in reality, is that by which the wills and actions of people are moderated through the removal of conflict and mutual interference between them by defining their boundaries.

Second: The individuals of a society governed by law are free in whatever lies beyond its scope, as necessitated by the human endowment of consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة) after they have been moderated. For this reason, contemporary laws do not interfere in the matters of divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) and ethics (الأخلاق). Consequently, these two vital matters are conceived in whatever form the law shapes them; they reconcile and harmonize with it according to the rule of subordination (التبعية). Thus, sooner or later, they turn into superficial rituals devoid of spiritual purity (الصفاء المعنوي). This is also the reason for what we witness of politics toying with religion: one day it suppresses and refutes it, another day it leans toward it and exaggerates in elevating its word, and yet another day it turns away from it and leaves it alone.

Third: This method is not free from defect. Although the law entrusts the guarantee of its implementation to the power concentrated in an individual or individuals, there is ultimately no guarantee for its implementation. This means that if the source of power and authority deviates from the truth, turning the authority of the collective over the collective into the authority of his own person over the collective, and the scales turn against the law, there is nothing to subdue this subduer and return him to the course of justice (العدل). There are many testimonies to this in what we have witnessed in our own time—which is an era of culture and civilization—let alone the countless historical testimonies. Add to this defect another: the violation of the law is sometimes hidden from the executive power, or it falls outside the domain of its capability. (Let us return to the beginning of the discussion).

In short, civil societies are united by a single goal, which is the enjoyment of the advantages of the worldly life (الحياة الدنيا)—this being "felicity" (السعادة) in their view. However, since Islam views human life as having a wider sphere than the material worldly life—indeed, within the sphere of its life is the life of the Hereafter, which is *the* life—and it sees that in this life nothing is of benefit except divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), which in its entirety resolves into monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*; and it sees that this gnosis cannot be preserved except through noble traits of character (مكارم الأخلاق) and the purity of the soul (طهارة النفس) from every vice; and it sees that these ethics are not perfected or completed except through a righteous social life based on the worship of Allah, the Glorified, and submission to what His Lordship (ربوبيته) necessitates, and dealing with people on the basis of social justice (العدل الاجتماعي)—Islam has taken the goal upon which human society is formed and united to be the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد). It then established the law on the foundation of monotheism. It did not stop at merely moderating wills and actions but completed it with acts of worship (العباديات) and added to them the true gnosis and virtuous ethics.

Then, it placed the guarantee of their implementation in the charge of the Islamic government first, and then in the charge of society second, through righteous upbringing (التربية الصالحة) in both knowledge and action, and through the enjoining of good (الأمر بالمعروف) and the forbidding of evil (النهي عن المنكر).

Among the most important things observed in this religion is the connection of all its parts—a connection that leads to a complete unity between them. This means that the spirit of monotheism (روح التوحيد) flows through the noble ethics to which this religion calls, and the spirit of ethics is spread throughout the actions required of the members of society. Thus, all parts of the Islamic religion, when analyzed, return to monotheism; and monotheism, when synthesized, becomes ethics and actions. If it were to descend, it would be them; and if they were to ascend, they would be it. To Him ascends the good word, and the righteous deed lifts it up.

If you say: The defect mentioned regarding civil laws—when the executive power rebels against implementing them or when violations are hidden from it—is the very same defect found in Islam. The clearest evidence for this is what we witness of the weakness of the religion and the disappearance of its authority over Islamic society, which is only due to its lack of those who would enforce its laws (نواميسه) upon the people today!

I say: The reality of general laws, whether divine or human, is nothing but mental images (صور ذهنية) in the minds of people and knowledge preserved in the hearts. They only enter the realm of action and reach the level of the senses through human wills (الإرادات الإنسانية) that attach to them. It is obvious that if the wills rebel, nothing will exist in external reality that corresponds to the laws. The matter, then, concerns that which preserves the attachment of these wills to actualization so that the laws may stand firm. Civil laws do not concern themselves with more than attaching actions to wills—meaning the will of the majority—and then they do not concern themselves with what preserves this will. As long as the will is alive, conscious, and active, the law is carried out by it. But if it dies due to a degradation that afflicts the souls of the people or a senility that strikes the structure of society; or if it is alive but has lost the quality of consciousness and perception because society is submerged in diversions and expanded in luxury and enjoyment; or if it is alive and conscious but has lost its influence due to the emergence of a superior, dominant, and despotic power that subdues its will to the will of the majority—then the law fails. Likewise, in incidents that the executive power has no way of knowing, such as secret crimes, or incidents over which it has no way to extend its authority, such as those outside its zone of influence—in all these cases, the nation does not achieve its wish for the implementation of the law and the preservation of society from corruption and disintegration. The major divisions occurring in European nations after the first and second Great World Wars are among the best examples in this regard.

This (meaning the violation of laws and the corruption and disintegration of society) is only because society did not concern itself with the cause that preserves the wills of the nation in their strength and authority, which is high ethics (الأخلاق العالية). For the will does not draw support for its survival and the continuation of its life except from the character (الخلق) suitable to it, as explained in psychology. If the established norm (السنة) in society were not settled and the law current within it were not based on a firm foundation of high ethics, it would be like a tree uprooted from the earth, having no stability.

Consider in this regard the emergence of Communism; it is nothing but a child of Democracy, produced by the luxury of one class of society and the deprivation of others. Thus, there was a vast distance between the two points of cruelty and lack of fairness, and resentment and the accumulation of rage and fury. Likewise, in the World Wars that occurred time and again and threaten humanity a third time—which have corrupted the earth and destroyed crops and progeny—there is no factor behind them except the instinct of arrogance (الاستكبار), greed, and avarice.

However, Islam built its current norm and established laws on the foundation of ethics and went to great lengths in training people upon them, because the laws governing actions are under their guarantee and in their charge. Thus, they are with the human being in his secret and his public, in his solitude and his company, performing their function and doing their work better than any monitoring policeman or any power that exerts its care in preserving order.

Yes, public education (المعارف العمومية) in these countries does care about training people in praiseworthy ethics and exerts its effort in urging and encouraging people toward them, but that does not benefit them at all.

Firstly, because the sole source of moral vices is nothing but excess and extravagance in material enjoyment and extreme deprivation therein. The laws have given people complete freedom in this, allowing some to enjoy and depriving others. Is the call to virtuous ethics and the encouragement toward them anything other than a call to two contradictory things or a request to combine two opposites?

Furthermore, these people, as you know, think with a social mindset (تفكرا اجتماعيا), yet their societies continue to exaggerate in the oppression of weak societies, the suppression of their rights, the enjoyment of what is in their hands, the enslavement of their souls, and the expansion of domination over them as much as they are able. The call to righteousness and godwariness (التقوى) alongside this characteristic is nothing but a contradictory call that remains sterile.

Secondly, because virtuous ethics also need a guarantor to ensure their preservation and protection in order to remain firm and stable, and this is nothing but monotheism (التوحيد)—meaning the statement that the world has one God, possessing the Most Beautiful Names, who created the creation for the purpose of their perfection and felicity, and who loves good and righteousness and hates evil and corruption, and who will gather everyone for the final judgment and the fulfillment of recompense, rewarding the doer of good for his goodness and the doer of evil for his evil. It is clear that without belief in the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment*, there would be no fundamental cause to deter one from following whims or to restrain the natural desires of the soul. For human nature desires and craves its own objects of desire, not what benefits another—as is the nature of any other individual—unless it returns in some way to its own desire (reflect well on this).

In a situation where a person, for instance, finds enjoyment in extinguishing a right belonging to another, and there is no deterrent to stop him, no recompenser to reward or punish him, and no blamer to rebuke him, what barrier would prevent him from committing the sin and perpetrating the injustice, no matter how great it may be? As for the various deterrents that are imagined—and in which researchers often err—such as attachment to the homeland, love of the species, fair praise, and the like, these are merely emotional sentiments and internal inclinations that have no preserving cause except instruction and training, without being based on a necessitating cause. They are, therefore, accidental attributes and customary matters with no barrier to prevent their disappearance. Why should a person sacrifice himself for another so that the other may enjoy life after him, while he sees death as annihilation and nullity? Fair praise is only on the tongues of others, and there is no pleasure for the one who sacrificed himself after the nullification of his essence.

In short, the insightful thinker does not doubt that a person does not undertake a deprivation that does not return to him as a reward or benefit. That which promises and entices him in these cases with the survival of "good mention," "eternal fair praise," and "pride that remains as long as time" is nothing but a delusion by which he is deceived and a trick by which he is misled through the excitement of his feelings and emotions. It is imagined to him that after his death and the nullification of his essence, his state will be like his state before death, so he feels his "good mention" and takes pleasure in it. This is nothing but an error of the imagination (غلط الوهم), like a drunkard who is captivated by the excitement of his senses, so he pardons and gives of himself, his honor, his wealth, or every dignity he has, which he would not undertake if he were sober and rational. He is a drunkard who does not reason, yet he considers that "chivalry" (فتوة) when it is folly and madness.

These slips and their like have no fortress for the human being to take refuge in except the monotheism (التوحيد) we mentioned. For this reason, Islam established noble ethics and made them part of its current way based on monotheism, among whose implications is the belief in the Return (المعاد). Its necessity is that the human being commits to goodness and avoids evil wherever he is and whenever he is, whether people know of it or not, whether a praiser praises him or not, and whether there is someone with him to compel him or deter him or not. For with him is Allah, the All-Knowing, the All-Preserving, the Maintainer of every soul as to what it has earned; and behind him is a day when every soul will find what it has done of good presented, and what it has done of evil, and therein every soul will be recompensed for what it has earned.

**7 - Two Logics: The Logic of Intellection and the Logic of Sensation:**
As for the logic of sensation (منطق الإحساس), it calls toward worldly benefit and incites toward it. If an action is accompanied by a benefit and the person senses it, the sensation is ignited and intensely longing in its incitement and movement. But if the person does not sense the benefit, it is extinguished and lifeless. As for the logic of intellection (منطق التعقل), it only incites toward following the truth (الحق) and sees that it is the best thing by which a person benefits, whether he senses a material benefit with the action or not; for what is with Allah is better and more lasting. Compare in this regard the saying of Antarah, which is based on the logic of sensation:

*And my saying whenever [my soul] gasped and surged:*
*Stay in your place; you shall be praised or you shall find rest.*

He means: I steady my soul whenever it trembles in the tremors and terrifying positions of combat by saying to it: "Be firm; for if you are killed, people will praise you for your firmness and lack of retreat, and if you kill the enemy, you will find rest and attain your desire." Thus, firmness is good in any case. Contrast this with the saying of the Exalted—which is based on the logic of intellection:

**قُلْ لَنْ يُصِيبَنا إِلَّا ما كَتَبَ اللهُ لَنا هُوَ مَوْلانا وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَنْ يُصِيبَكُمُ اللهُ بِعَذابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينا فَتَرَبَّصُوا إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ مُتَرَبِّصُونَ**
Say, ‘Nothing will befall us except what Allah has ordained for us. He is our master, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust.’ Say, ‘Do you await for us anything except one of the two best outcomes? But we await for you that Allah shall visit on you a punishment from Himself or by our hands. So wait! We too are waiting with you.’ (9:51-52)

It means that the matter of our guardianship and victory belongs to Allah, the Glorified; we do not desire in anything that befalls us of good or evil except what He has promised us of reward for submitting (الإسلام) to Him and committing to His religion, as the Exalted said:

**لا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ وَلا نَصَبٌ وَلا مَخْمَصَةٌ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَطَؤُنَ مَوْطِئاً يَغِيظُ الْكُفَّارَ وَلا يَنالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَيْلاً إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ بِهِ عَمَلٌ صالِحٌ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ وَلا يُنْفِقُونَ نَفَقَةً صَغِيرَةً وَلا كَبِيرَةً وَلا يَقْطَعُونَ وادِياً إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللهُ أَحْسَنَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ**
...they do not suffer thirst, fatigue, or hunger in the way of Allah, nor do they take any step that enrages the faithless, nor do they inflict any loss on an enemy but a righteous deed is written down for them on its account. Indeed Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous. And they do not spend any expenditure, small or great, nor do they cross any valley, but it is written down for them, so that Allah may reward them with the best of what they used to do. (9:120-121)

If it is so, then if you kill us or something from you befalls us, we shall have a great reward and a beautiful end with our Lord; and if we kill you or something from us befalls you, we shall have a great reward, a beautiful end, and empowerment in the world over our enemy. Thus, in any case, we are felicitous and envied. You do not offer us in our fighting, nor do you await for us in our affair, except one of the two best outcomes. We are upon goodness and felicity in any case, while you are upon "felicity" and the attainment of desire according to your belief in only one of the two possibilities—and that is in one of the two states, which is when the turn is yours against us. Thus, we await for you what will grieve you, while you await for us nothing but what gladdens us and makes us felicitous.

These are two logics. One of them means firmness and lack of cessation based on a sensory foundation, which is that the one who is firm has one of two benefits: either the praise of people or rest from the enemy. This is if there is a benefit returning to the fighting person who throws himself into destruction. But if there is no returning benefit—such as if people do not praise him because they do not appreciate the value of struggle (الجهاد) and service and treachery are equal to them; or if the service is of a nature that will never appear to them, nor will the treachery; or if the sensation does not find rest in the annihilation of the enemy but rather the truth (الحق) finds rest in it—then this logic has nothing but impotence and stammering.

These enumerated cases are the general causes for every transgression, treachery, and crime. The traitor who is negligent in the matter of the law says: his service is not appreciated by people with what equals it, and the servant and the traitor are equal to them—rather, the traitor is in a better state and a more pleasant life. Every transgressor and criminal sees that he will escape the subdual of the law and that the monitoring powers are unable to catch him, so his affair remains hidden and his person is obscured to the people. Everyone who hesitates and is heavy in establishing the truth and revolting against its enemies, and who compromises with them, excuses himself by saying that standing for the truth humiliates him among people, and the present world laughs at him, and they count him among the remnants of the Middle Ages or the eras of myths. If you remind him of the nobility of the soul (شرافة النفس) and the purity of the interior (طهارة الباطن), he retorts: "What do I do with the nobility of the soul if it leads to a miserable life and a humiliated existence?"

As for the other logic, which is the logic of Islam, it builds its foundation on following the truth and seeking reward and recompense from Allah, the Glorified. The purpose only attaches to worldly goals and objectives in the subsequent rank and as a secondary intention (بالقصد الثاني). It is known that no case is excluded from its encompassment, nor does its universality fall from generality and consistency. The action—whether it be doing or refraining—only occurs for His sake, in submission (إسلاما) to Him and in following the truth that He willed. And He is the All-Preserving, the All-Knowing, whom neither slumber nor sleep overtakes, and there is no protector from Him, and nothing is hidden from Him in the earth or in the heaven, and Allah is aware of what you do.

Thus, over every soul, in whatever action it enters or issues, there is a watchful witness (رقيب شهيد) maintaining what it has earned, whether people witness it or not, praise it or not, or are capable of anything regarding it or not.

The beautiful effect of Islamic training reached such a point that people would come to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and confess their crimes and offenses to him in repentance (توبة), tasting the bitterness of the legal punishments (الحدود) carried out upon them—execution and below—seeking the pleasure of Allah and purifying their souls from the filth of sins and the stain of evil deeds. By reflecting on these rare occurrences, the researcher can transition to the wondrous effect of religious discourse on the souls of people and its habituating them to generosity in the most delicious and dearest things to them, which is life and what follows it. Were it not that the discussion is Quranic, we would have cited some historical examples of this.

**8 - What is the meaning of seeking reward with Allah and turning away from others?**
One might imagine that making the otherworldly reward the general purpose in human social life necessitates the collapse of the vital purposes toward which the natural human structure calls, and therein lies the corruption of the social order and a descent into the depths of monasticism (الرهبانية). How can one be devoted to one objective while preserving other important objectives? Is this not a contradiction?

But this is a delusion arising from ignorance of divine wisdom and the secrets revealed by Quranic gnosis. For Islam builds its legislation on the principle of creation (أصل التكوين), as mentioned repeatedly in previous discussions of this book. The Exalted said:

**فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ**
So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah on which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion... (30:30)

The summary is: the chain of real existential causes (الأسباب الواقعية التكوينية) cooperated to bring the human species into existence at its end and worked to drive it toward the vital goal prepared for it. It is therefore necessary for the human being to build his life, in the context of striving and choice, upon harmony with the causes in what they want from him and drive him toward, so that his life does not contradict them, leading him to destruction and wretchedness. This (if the deluded one would understand it) is the Islamic religion itself. Since there is above the causes a single Cause who is their Originator and the Disposer of their affairs in the minute and the grand—and that is Allah, the Glorified, who is the Complete Cause (السبب التام) above every cause in the full sense of the word—it was obligatory for the human being to submit (الإسلام) to Him and be humble before His command. This is the meaning of monotheism (التوحيد) being the sole foundation of the Islamic religion.

From here it becomes clear that preserving the word of monotheism, submitting to Allah, and seeking His face in life proceeds in harmony with all causes, giving every possessor of a right its due without polytheism (شرك) or heedlessness. Thus, the Muslim individual has worldly goals and objectives and otherworldly ones; he has material purposes and spiritual ones. But he does not give them more care and attention than is appropriate. For that very reason, we see that Islam calls to the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, devotion to Him, sincerity for Him, and turning away from every cause besides Him and every seeker of other than Him; yet at the same time, it commands people to follow the laws of life and proceed according to natural courses.

From here it becomes clear that the members of the Islamic society are the felicitous ones with the reality of felicity in this world and the Hereafter, and that their goal—which is seeking the face of Allah in actions—does not conflict with other vital goals when they appear and are prioritized.

From here also becomes clear the falsity of another delusion mentioned by a group of sociologists: that the reality of religion and its original purpose is the establishment of social justice (العدالة الاجتماعية), and acts of worship are branches derived from it; thus, whoever establishes it is upon the religion even if he does not clothe himself in a creed or servitude.

The researcher who reflects on the Book and the Sunnah, and especially on the Prophetic biography (السيرة النبوية), does not need extra effort or labored argumentation to realize the falsity of this delusion. This talk, which involves dropping monotheism and noble ethics from the collection of religious laws, involves returning the religious goal—which is the word of monotheism—to the civil goal, which is enjoyment. You have already learned that they are two different goals; one does not return to the other, neither in its origin nor in its branches and fruits.

**9 - What is the meaning of freedom in Islam?**
The word "freedom" (الحرية), in the sense intended by it, has not exceeded a few centuries in its circulation on tongues. Perhaps the cause that innovated it was the European civil renaissance a few centuries ago, but its meaning was present in minds and was one of the wishes of hearts since ancient times.

The natural existential principle (الأصل الطبيعي التكويني) from which this meaning springs is the human endowment in his existence of the will (الإرادة) that incites him to action. For it is a psychological state whose nullification is the nullification of sense and consciousness, leading to the nullification of humanity.

However, since the human being is a social being whose nature drives him to live in society and cast his bucket among the buckets by entering his will into other wills and his action into other actions—which leads to submission to a law that moderates wills and actions by placing limits on them—the very nature that gave him the release of will and action is the same nature that limits will and action and restricts that initial release and primary freedom.

Contemporary civil laws, since they established their rulings on the basis of material enjoyment, produced the freedom of the nation in the matter of original religious gnosis in terms of commitment to it and its necessities, and in the matter of ethics, and in whatever lies beyond the laws of all that the human being wants and chooses of wills and actions. This is what is meant by freedom according to them.

As for Islam, it established its law on the foundation of monotheism (التوحيد), then in the next rank on the foundation of virtuous ethics. Then it addressed every small and significant matter of individual and social actions, whatever they may be. There is nothing concerning the human being or that the human being concerns himself with except that the Islamic law has a foot or a footprint in it. Thus, there is no room or manifestation for freedom in the aforementioned sense within it.

Yes, for the human being therein is freedom from the bond of servitude to other than Allah, the Glorified.
